coredata.dc:description: Carbon aerogels are prepared by a thermal treating-freeze drying approach from chitosan, with glycine hydrochloride ionic liquid (IL) acting as solvent and nitrogen source. Different post-treatments such as ball milling and high temperature carbonization are employed to functionalize the obtained carbon aerogels with tuned properties, making it promising candidates as fluorescence material (NACs-Q), electrode material (FDC-800) and catalyst support (NACPd-C). NACs-Q is water-soluble quantum dot with average particle sizes of 3.8 nm, presenting excitation-/emission-independent and pH-sensitive properties, which could be used as sensor for testing acetone vapor or an “on-off-on” sensor for detections of Fe3+ and vitamin C in fruits. FDC-800 exhibits fluffy lamellar structure with developed micro-mesopores and nitrogen-containing groups on their surfaces, which is beneficial for building flexible solid-state supercapacitor with excellent performance, delivering a capacitance of 208F/g at 0.5 A/g, and achieving an energy density of 7.2 W h/kg at a power density of 50 W/kg. Moreover, NACPd-C can be used as catalyst for phenol hydrogenation, and phenol conversion of 100% with cyclohexanone selectivity of 98.3% is achieved, due to the synergetic effects of the Pd active-site, the N-containing groups, and the Lewis acid sites on the support.

FT-IR spectra of NCs-Q and NACs-Q (a), High-resolution C1s peaks (b), High-resolution N1s peaks (c), High-resolution O1s peaks (d); PL emission spectrum of NACs-Q/PVA composite film under H2O vapor and AC vapor (e), PL emission spectrum of NACs-Q/PVA composite film in presence of AC with various concentrations (f), the relationship between PL emission intensity and AC concentration (g) and UV–vis absorption spectrum NACs-Q/PVA composite film in presence of AC with various concentrations (h) and NACs-Q/PVA composite film under excitation by sunlight and UV light (i).

The relative fluorescent intensities (F/F0) of NACs-Q in presence of various metal ions with concentration of 1 mM (a), relative fluorescent intensities (F/F0) of NACs-Q in presence of various metal ions and Fe3+ (b), PL emission spectrum of NACs-Q in presence of Fe3+ with various concentrations (c), the relationship between PL emission intensity and Fe3+ concentration (d), Fluorescence decay curves of NACs-Q in absence and presence of Fe3+ (e), PL emission spectrum of NACs-Q in presence of Fe3+ and VC (f), and Schematic diagram of luminescence and quenching (g).
Electrochemical performance in 1 M H2SO4 in three-electrode system:

Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of FDC-700, FDC-800 and FDC-900 (a), Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and partial enlargement (b), corresponding pore size distribution curves (c), High-resolution N1s peaks (d), High-resolution O1s peaks (e) and Raman spectrum of FDC-800 (f).

Electrochemical performance in 1 M H2SO4 in two-electrode system: CV curves at different scan rates (a), GCD curves at different current densities (b), divided EDLC and pseudocapacitance area at 10 mV/s (c), Nyquist plots (d), Ragone plots (e), Cycling stability at 10 A/g for 10,000 cycles (f) of FDC-800, The relative CV curves of FDC-800 in response to the bending angle from 0°to 150°in symmetrical solid supercapacitor (g) and (h).
SEM images (a), TEM images (b) and HRTEM images (c) of NACs-C and Particle size distribution of Pd (d).
Pd XPS spectra (a), N1s XPS spectra (b), Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms (c), XRD patterns (d), Pyridine IR spectra (e) and NH3-TPD spectra (f) of NACpd-C.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon aerogels are prepared by a thermal treating-freeze drying approach from chitosan, with glycine hydrochloride ionic liquid. Different post-treatments such as ball milling and high temperature carbonization are employed to functionalize the obtained carbon aerogels with tuned properties, making it promising candidates as fluorescence material (NACs-Q), electrode material (FDC-800) and catalyst support (NACPd-C).

Carbon aerogels are prepared by a thermal treating-freeze drying approach from chitosan, with glycine hydrochloride ionic liquid (IL) acting as solvent and nitrogen source. Different post-treatments such as ball milling and high temperature carbonization are employed to functionalize the obtained carbon aerogels with tuned properties, making it promising candidates as fluorescence material (NACs-Q), electrode material (FDC-800) and catalyst support (NACPd-C). NACs-Q is water-soluble quantum dot with average particle sizes of 3.8 nm, presenting excitation-/emission-independent and pH-sensitive properties, which could be used as sensor for testing acetone vapor or an “on-off-on” sensor for detections of Fe3+ and vitamin C in fruits. FDC-800 exhibits fluffy lamellar structure with developed micro-mesopores and nitrogen-containing groups on their surfaces, which is beneficial for building flexible solid-state supercapacitor with excellent performance, delivering a capacitance of 208F/g at 0.5 A/g, and achieving an energy density of 7.2 W h/kg at a power density of 50 W/kg. Moreover, NACPd-C can be used as catalyst for phenol hydrogenation, and phenol conversion of 100% with cyclohexanone selectivity of 98.3% is achieved, due to the synergetic effects of the Pd active-site, the N-containing groups, and the Lewis acid sites on the support.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon aerogel is a promising nano-carbon material ranking the second popular aerogel after the inorganic aerogels, which exhibits attractive characters such as lightweight, high specific surface area, porous amorphous structure, easily tailored reaction sites. Rather than simply the intrinsic properties of carbon materials like good conductivity, high temperature resistance, and acid/alkali resistance. Such features make carbon aerogels have broad applications in mechanics, electricity, heat, light and other fields [1–4]. In addition, as the only conductive aerogel, carbon aerogels are excellent alternative for using as electrode material, and their unique three-dimensional nano-network structures also enable them as good candidates for utilizations as varieties of carriers in different applications.

Traditional carbon aerogels are cross-linked structure of phenolic prepolymer obtained by condensation reaction of polyphenol and aldehyde, which usually occurs under conditions of acid and base catalyst. During this formation process, the involved solvents easily volatilize and sublimate, leading to destructions of the formed skeleton structure of the aerogels [5,6]. Organic solvents are employed to prepare the aerogels for mitigating the aforementioned defects, and the resultant carbon aerogel presents good cross-linking and compact structure, but this approach inevitably increases the cost and bring negative impact on the environment at the same time [7]. With the deepening of human's need for green chemistry and cognition of renewable energy, using renewable and environmental-friendly biomass as raw material to prepare functional carbon materials has become a trend and shows promising potentials in different areas. Carbon aerogels with low density and developed pores are successfully prepared from biomass materials including cellulose, lignin, bamboo and bagasse, etc. The bio-based carbon aerogels with tailored characters and functions have unfolded as potential alternatives in a wide range of applications such as biological and medical fields [2,8].

Chitosan is generally produced from natural polysaccharide chitin by removing part of the acetyl groups, reserving the good biodegradability and biocompatibility natural characters. Chitosan poses a six-membered ring structure similar to cellulose, and neighboring molecule chains are connected to each other to form a relatively large range of two-dimensional layer polymer, which is consequently easy to build a three-dimensional carbon aerogel material after carbonization [9]. Additionally, chitosan contains large amounts of free ammonia, and N-self-doping can be realized during carbonization by incorporating N into carbon skeleton for forming stable nitrogen-containing groups [10]. However, chitosan with strong intermolecular and intramolecular hydrogen bond connections is difficult to dissolve in water, which limits its reaction and application in aqueous systems.

Studies have pointed out that ionic liquids improve the solubility of chitosan and promote further reactions by breaking the intermolecular and intramolecular hydrogen bonds [11]. and it was documented by our group that ionic liquids containing cationic amino acids presents better performance in dissolving chitosan than that of imidazoles, which is mainly attributed to that the molecular structure of amino acids containing carboxyl groups could form hydrogen bonds with NH2 and OH between and/or within chitosan molecules, thus reducing the intermolecular or intramolecular connections [12]. What is more, this kind of ionic liquid could, at the same time, provide additional N element to functionalize the obtained carbon aerogels.

Based on above analysis, in this study, chitosan-based functional carbon aerogels are prepared by using glycine hydrochloride ionic liquid as the solvent, and the as prepared functional aerogels exhibited multiple applications as carbon quantum dots, electrode material and catalyst support respectively. This study provides a new direction for preparation and application of biomass-based carbon aerogels.

All the chemicals were used without further purification, and their detailed information are listed below: Chitosan (degree of deacetylation ≥ 95%, Mw ≈ 50,000, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.,), Poly(vinyl alcohol) (degree of polymerization = 1700, Aladdin, degree of alcoholysis = 88%), Glycine Hydrochloride (IL, 9 Ding Chemistry, 99%), Acetone (C3H6O, Amresco, 99.5%), Sodium chloride (NaCl, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., 99.5%), Nickel chloride (NiCl2·6H2O, Sigma-Aldrich, 98.0%), Citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7·H2O, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., 99.5%), Sodium phosphate monobasic dihytrate (NaH2PO4·2H2O, Tianjin Bodi Chemical Co., Ltd., 99%), Ascorbic acid (C6H8O6, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., 99.7%), Potassium chloride (KCl, Macklin, 99.5%), Iron chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., 99%), Silver nitrate (AgNO3, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., 99.8%), Copper chloride (CuCl2·2H2O, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., 99%), Barium chloride dehydrate (BaCl2·2H2O, Aladdin, 99.5%), Copper chloride (CuCl2, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., 98%), Magnesium chloride hexahydrate (MgCl2·6H2O, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., 99%), Chromic chloride (CrCl3, Macklin, 99.5%), Stannous chloride dihydrate (SnCl2·2H2O, Macklin, 98%), Phenol (C6H6O, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., 98%), Dichloromethane (CH2Cl2, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., 99.5%), Palladium Chloride (PdCl2, 9 Ding Chemistry, 99%), Sodium borahydride (NaBH4, Aladdin, 96%).

4 g chitosan and 80 mL deionized water was added into a three flask, and the mixture was heated to 60 °C during constantly fast stirring, then 3 g glycine hydrochloride ionic liquid was added into the mixture slowly, keep stirring at 60 °C for 2 h to obtain a bright yellow liquid. Then it was heated up to 160 °C and reacting for 4 h to obtain chitosan colloid carbon. The obtained colloid carbon was freeze-drying for 24 h after frozen in refrigerator for 48 h, the resultant solid was chitosan-based aerogels and named as NACs, those obtained without ionic liquid were marked as NCs.
a)
Fluorescentmaterials

The NACs was ball-milling for 2 h at a rotational speed of 600 rpm using a planetary ball mill (Across International, PQ-N04), with two 100 mL stainless steel jars and stainless balls (φ5:φ10 = 10:1, 224 g per jar), the mass ratio of ball to NACs was 50:1. The obtained carbon quantum dots are water-soluble and can be easily dissolved into deionized water to form quantum dots solution, which was named as NACs-Q, and those obtained without ionic liquid were marked as NCs-Q.
b)
Electrode materials

NACs was then carbonized at 700, 800 and 900 °C for 2 h under CO2 atmosphere for further use as electrode and it was labelled as FDC-T, where T represents the temperature employed for carbonization. FDC-T, Super P and polytetrafluoroethylene were blended at a ratio of 80:10:10, and then ground and press on the Ti mesh to act as the working electrode.
c)
Catalyst support

NACs was impregnated in PdCl2 solution (10 mol/L) for 24 h. After drying, the obtained solid products were co-carbonization at 800 °C for 2 h under N2 atmosphere. After that, the carbonized material was reduced in NaBH4 to obtain the catalyst, named as NACPd-C, while those without PdCl2 doping were named as NAC-C.

The surface morphology and microstructure images were obtained by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S-4800, Japan) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL 2011, the Netherlands). UV–vis absorbance spectra were obtained by a UV1006M031 UV–vis spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectra were obtained by F-7000 fluorescent spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan), with a voltage of 400 V. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) of samples were obtained on a Fourier spectrometer (Fourier, PerkinElmer, American). The binding energies were evaluated with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; PHI5700, MN, USA). The fluorescence lifetime of carbon dots was recorded on a FLS980 lifetime spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms were performed at −196 °C (ASAP 2020, Micromeritics, USA). The specific surface areas and the porous size distribution were tested using Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) and density function theory (DFT) method, respectively. Raman spectra were recorded with a Raman spectrometer (In via, Renishaw, UK) with Argon ion laser excitation of 514 nm. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded with a D/Max-rB diffractometer (Rigaku, Tokyo, Japan) using Cu Kα radiation (45 kV, 40 mA, λ = 1.54178 Å) with a scan range of 2θ = 5–80°. Pyridine IR spectra were obtained with a Nicolet 380 spectrometer (Thermo, USA) at a resolution of 4 cm−1 over 32 scans. Temperature-programmed desorption of ammonia (NH3-TPD) was used to determine sample acidity (RS232, Micromeritics).

Stability test under UV irradiation: the NACs solution was placed in a dark environment, irradiated with 365 nm UV lamp for 60 min, and samples were taken every 10 min during the irradiation to measure the fluorescence intensity.

Stability test in salt solutions with different concentrations: NaCl salt solutions with a concentration gradient of 0–800 mM were prepared, take same amount of NACs solutions to mix with salt solutions of different concentrations, balanced for 10 min, then test their fluorescence intensity under excitation at 340 nm.

Buffer solutions with pH = 2–12 were prepared using disodium hydrogen phosphate and citric acid. The same amount of NACs solutions was placed in different pH conditions, balanced for 10 min, and the fluorescence intensity was tested under excitation at 340 nm.

3 g of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) was mixed with 27 mL of deionized water under continuous stirring in a water bath until the PVA was completely dissolved. 1 mL of dialyzed NACs solution (5 mg/mL) was mixed with 9 mL of PVA aqueous solution to ensure that the quantum dots were completely dispersed. After mixing evenly, the solutions were dropped in a petri dish, then placed in an oven at 60 °C for 3–4 h to obtain the NACs/PVA composite membrane material.

NACs/PVA composite membrane and 20 mL of AC solution (0–1000 μM) were placed in a vacuum dryer, and after 2 h, photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of NACs/PVA were tested. Another group using 20 mL water instead of acetone was used as control.

The detection of metal ions was carried out in aqueous solution at room temperature. Various metal ion solutions of the same concentration were prepared and then 2 mL (5 mg/mL) of NACs solution was added, respectively. The mixed solutions were equilibrium for 10 min before fluorescence measurement, and the emission spectra were recorded with a fluorescence spectrophotometer with excitation wavelength of 340 nm to detect the selectivity of metal ions. In order to evaluate their sensitivity to Fe3+, the same amount of NACs solution was added into the Fe3+ solution with concentration of 0–1000 nM, then the emission spectra with excitation wavelength of 340 nm was recorded.

Electrochemical tests for three-electrode, two-electrode and solid-state system were all performed in 1 M H2SO4 electrolyte using CHI 660E workstation. For three-electrode system, Ag/AgCl electrode was used as the reference electrode and a platinum plate as the counter electrode. For two-electrode system and the solid-state supercapacitors, the obtained samples worked as both anode and cathode with symmetric quality. Electrochemical tests including galvanostatic charge-discharge cycling (GCD), cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The potential windows were −0.1–0.9 V and 0–1 V in the three-electrode and two-electrode tests at the CV test respectively. Cycling stability was measured on a Land CT3001A test system at a current density of 10 A/g. The gravimetric capacitances (Cg, F/g) were calculated based on the GCD curves. In the three-electrode system, the Cg were calculated through the following equations (1):

Phenol (0.141 g), ACNpd (0.266 g) and dichloromethane(6 mL) were placed in a 40 mL autoclave, which was purged with H2 several times. The reactions were performed at different reaction temperatures and H2 pressures for different durations with stirring at 600 rpm. After reaction, the reaction was quenched to prevent further progress. The liquid in the reactor was collected by centrifugal separation for further analysis. The used catalyst was collected and washed for further cycling tests.

The products were determined by gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) using previously reported conditions [14]. Before analysis, all the samples were filtered using a 0.22 μm membrane filter. Conversion rates and selectivity were quantitatively calculated using toluene as an internal standard according to the following formula:

Chitosan is usually insoluble in water, herein, a “green solvent” ionic liquid- glycine hydrochloride is introduced, which can increase the solubility of chitosan and provide extra nitrogen at the same time. From preliminary study in our research group [12]. the NH2 and OH could interact with H+ in solution, destroying the hydrogen bond with strong binding force in the ordered structure of chitosan and promoting its dissolution ability. Moreover, glycine hydrochloride also provides Cl− that is capable of forming hydrogen bonds with the hydroxyl group on the chitosan molecular chain, further weakening the hydrogen bonds between intermolecular and intramolecular [13–14]. Besides, then dehydration condensation could occur between chitosan and glycine, forming NHCO during the reaction (Fig. 1 a).

From TEM analysis in Fig. 1b and c, the obtained NACs-Q is soluble spherical particles with obvious lattice fringes, and the spacing is 0.22 nm that attributed to the (100) lattice of graphite carbon [15]. The particle sizes are in the range of 2–6 nm, with an average size of only 3.8 nm. Such ultrasmall size endows NACs-Q improved quantum properties. NACs-Q aqueoussolution displays pale yellow under sunlight, while the solution exhibit green luminescence on 254 nm UV irradiation, and blue luminescence on 365 nm UV irradiation as shown in Fig. 1e (inset). UV–vis and PL behavior are investigated to explore the optical properties of NACs-Q. From UV–vis spectrum in Fig. 1e, two obvious absorption peaks at 240 nm and 295 nm can be observed, corresponding to π-π* conjugated aromatic system and n-π* transition of CO/CN groups respectively [16]. A maximum emission peak centered at 432 nm appears with the excitation wavelength of 340 nm (Fig. 1f). Two peaks at 340 nm and 268 nm are detected in the characteristic excitation spectrum, which is in consistence with the UV–vis absorption spectrum. The excitation wavelengths have direct influence on emission wavelength and intensity. From Fig. 1g, when increasing excitation wavelength from 300 to 400 nm, the intensity emission peaks increase first and then decrease with peak red-shifting from 390 to 500 nm, indicating the characteristic of excitation-dependence for NACs-Q. The PL properties can be attributed to surface energy traps formed by an incomplete reaction and the radiative recombination of electrons and holes [17]. The PL of NACs-Q aqueous solution with different pH is shown in Fig. 1h, and there is no shift of the characteristic peak. However, obvious changes regarding to the peak intensity is observed, indicating NACs-Q is pH-independent. The PL peak intensity reaches its highest value in solution of pH = 2. There are abundant oxygen-containing groups on the surface of NACs-Q to endow acidic properties. When increasing the pH of solution, their negative charge density accumulating on the surface, affecting the π-th* and n-e* electron transfer, leading to the solution color changes and fluorescence quenching [18].

FT-IR and XPS are conducted to characterize the structure and composition of NACs-Q and further analyze their PL characteristics. Fig. 2 a shows the normalized FT-IR spectra of NCs-Q and NACs-Q, the peak centered at 1522 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibrations of aromatic CC, and the peak at 1021 cm−1 corresponds to out-of-plane bending vibration of aromatic CH, demonstrating the formation of aromatic structure and indicating NACs-Q is consisting of polyaromatic skeleton [19]. The adsorption peaks centered at 1635 cm−1, 1409 cm−1 and 1319 cm−1 representing for CO, NH and CN respectively, which exhibit peak shifts after IL added, indicating reactions occurred between chitosan and IL [20,21]. Compared with the sample without the presence of IL (NCs-Q), the peak intensity of CO and NH increase obviously for NACs-Q, pointing out the occurrence of dehydration condensation reaction and the formation of amido bond, which help to promote n-π* transition and contribute to the fluorescence emission [22]. Furthermore, stretching vibrations of CO are observed, and the results imply that NACs-Q presents polyaromatic structure with abundant oxygen and nitrogen-containing groups on their surfaces.

Three peaks representing for C, N and O are detected in XPS spectrum (Figure S1), further confirming the existence of the nitrogen-and oxygen-containing groups on the surface of NACs-Q. The high resolution C1s XPS in Fig. 2b can be deconvoluted into four peaks at 288.9, 288.1, 286.2 and 284.7 eV respectively, which attributed to OCO/CO, CO, CN and CC/CC groups [23]. N1s in Fig. 2(c) displays three peaks at 402.2, 401.3, 399.9 and 399.1 eV, representing for oxidized N (CNO, N-X), quaternary N (N-C3, N-Q), Pyrrolic N (CNC, N-5) and pyridinic N (CNC, N-6). Quaternary N is the dominated form of nitrogen, indicating the high graphitization degree of NACs-Q [24]. O1s peaks is deconvoluted into two peaks at 532.8 and 531.1 eV, attributed to CO and CO/COOH bands [25]. which is in consistent with FT-IR analysis. The existing N and O-containing groups can effectively promote electron conjugate with aromatic structure for fluorescence radiation.

To test the luminescence stabilityof NACs-Q, a series studies are done under UV radiation and in NaCl salt solution as shown in Figure S2. After irradiation under UV for 60 min, the fluorescence intensity only shows a slightly decrease, demonstrating their photostability. When mixing with salt solution at various concentrations, the samples present few changes for the fluorescence intensity even increasing the NaCl concentration up to 800 mM, which is attributed to their ultra-small sizes and the abundant hydroxyl groups on the surface and electrostatic repulsion between the particles [26]. These stabilities make NACs-Q aqueous solutions capable of being stored at room temperature for several months without significant aggregation or fluorescent degradation occurrence.

When adding NACs-Q solution into polyvinylalcohol to prepare NACs-Q/PVA film, the composite film exhibits blue fluorescence when irradiated under 365 nm UV light, as shown in Fig. 2(i). Compared with exposure to H2O vapor, the fluorescence intensity obviously ramps up after exposure with acetone vapor. As a result, the composite film is therefore employed as a sensor for acetone vapor detection. Fig. 2(f) exhibits the fluorescence spectrum of NACs-Q/PVA with different acetone vapor concentrations. The fluorescence intensity increases when increasing acetone concentration from 0 to 1000 μM. A good linear relationship between acetone concentration and fluorescence intensity is obtained, with a correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.9938 after curves fitting with AC in the range of 0–100 μM. The liner equation is F = 2298.04 + 1.9925[AC], and the detection limitation is 1.44 μM. The ultravioletspectrum of NACs-Q/PVA with different acetone vapor concentrations are shown in Fig. 2(g). The characteristic adsorption peak of the film at c.a. 300 nm disappears gradually when increasing the acetone concentration, accompanied by the emerging of a new adsorption peak at 260 nm, implying new compounds are produced resulted in the changes of fluorescence intensity (Fig. 2h). These aforementioned analyses provide theoretical foundations for the carbon dots utilizing as sensors. In addition, the composite film can be used as solid-state luminescence sensors to detect the highly volatile AC vapors. Compared with conventional liquid sensors, the proposed solid-state sensors present more extensive practical value for their portability.

The presence of large number of nitrogen-containing groups on the surface of NACs-Q gives the carbon dots positive charged. Additionally, the existed oxygen and nitrogen functional groups on the surface of NACs-Q can interact with metal ions to form complexes by coordination, which indicates that NACs-Q has great potential to act as a probe for detecting metal ions. Here various metal ions including K+, Fe3+, Na+, Ag+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Cr3+, Ni2+, Sn2+and Ba2+ are selected to explore their influences on PL properties of NACs-Q. Fig. 3 (a) shows the relative fluorescence intensities of NACs-Q in the presence of various metal ions at concentration of 1 mM. NACs-Q exhibits the highest fluorescence quenching in presence of Fe3+, indicating the good selectivity for Fe3+ than other ions. To confirm the quenching effect of Fe3+, as shown in Fig. 3(b), 1 mM Fe3+ was added into above various metal ions solution. Compared with the addition of single metal ions (black bar), extra added Fe3+ makes the PL intensity dramatically decreased (red bar), proving NACs-Q has a higher selectivity to Fe3+ than other coexisting metal ions. The functional groups such as NH2, OH on the surface of NACs-Q are electron-rich groups, which can interact with Fe3+ via high binding affinity. When Fe3+ is added into aqueous solution, they chelate with the OH and NH2 to form the NACs-Q/Fe3+ aggregation. During this coordinated interaction, electron in the excited state of NACs-Q would transfer to the unsaturated orbit of Fe3+, leading to nonradiative electron/hole recombination, and the destruction of the radiation transition causes a fluorescence quenching of the NACs-Q [27]. The PL spectrum of NACs-Q with different Fe3+ concentrations is shown in Fig. 3(c). The fluorescence intensity decreases when increasing the Fe3+ ion concentration, which demonstrating the carbon dots are sensitive to the Fe3+ concentration. Fig. 3(d) presents a good liner relationship between Fe3+ concentration and fluorescence intensity. Based on Stern-Volmer equation:

, F0 is the initial fluorescence intensity of NACs-Q without Fe3+, F is the fluorescence intensity after added Fe3+, KSV denotes the quenching constant, C represents the concentration of Fe3+ [28]. The liner relation is described as F0/F = 0.99681 + 0.00145[Fe3+] at the range of 0–200 μM with R2 = 0.9973. Limit of detection is 0.89 μM calculated from

, where σ is the standard deviation of fluorescence emission intensity.

Fluorescence lifetime is an important parameter to assess the quenching mechanism, and the corresponding lifetime before and after Fe3+ adding is 5.91 and 5.72 ns respectively. Calculated by Kqτ0 = Ksv
[29]. Kq is larger than the typical quenching constant for fluorescent molecules of (1.0–2.0) × 1010 L·mol−1·s−1, indicating quenching occurred via a static quenching mechanism. Further adding some vitamin C into the quenching solution, the quenched fluorescence is partially recoveried as shown in Fig. 3f. VC is added directly into NACs-Q solution to elucidate its role. The PL intensity barely changed indicates that VC has no direct influence on PL of NACs-Q, thus the fluorescence recovery of NACs-Q-Fe3+ solution is ascribed to redox reaction between Fe3+ and VC, where Fe3+ is reduced to Fe2+ and hydroxyl on VC is oxidized to ketone. The Fe2+ exhibits a lower chelating ability to NACs-Q than that of Fe3+, as a result, thefluorescence intensity of NACs-Q-Fe3+ solution is partially reduced. Based on this rapid fluorescence quenching and recovery response, NACs-Q can be used as an “on-off-on” sensor for the detection of Fe3+ and vitamin C in fruits (the schematic diagram was listed in Fig. 3g). This new method has potential advantages over traditional chromatography approaches.

After high-temperature carbonization, the obtained FDC-T exhibits porous structure with developed nitrogen-containing groups on the surfaces, which enables its potential application using as electrode material in supercapacitor, thus the FDC-T was assembled into a three-electrode system using H2SO4 as electrolyte, as shown in Fig. 4 , to evaluate their electrochemical performance.The CV curves of the FDC-800 and FDC-900 in Fig. 4a exhibit good rectangle-like shape at 10 mV/s, which is the typical characteristic of carbon materials, indicating the good electricaldouble layercapacitor (EDLC) of the two samples [30]. The curve of FDC-700 is an irregular triangle, indicating its poor electrochemical performance. An obvious redox peak in 0.1–0.6 V is observed for FDC-800 and FDC-900, pointing out the existence of pseudocapacitance, which mainly derived from the nitrogen and oxygen-containing groups on surface of chitosan.

The GCD curves in Fig. 4(b) allshow a symmetrical isosceles triangle, illustrating their good reversibility of charge and discharge. The specific capacities are calculated to be 109 F/g, 269 F/g and 106 F/g at 0.2 A/g for FDC-700, FDC-800 and FDC-900 respectively, based on the GCD curves. FDC-800 owns the highest capacity when used as electrode, the excellent performance derived from the combination of EDLC and pseudocapacitance. Furthermore, FDC-800 also exhibits a favorable rate capability (Fig. 4c), with capacity being 269 F/g at 0.2/A g, and keeps 216 F/g at 10 A/g, superior than the values of FDC-700 and FDC-900, which are also higher than many other carbon materials reported in literatures [30,31]. The EIS of the three samples are shown in Fig. 4(d), and they all present straight lines at low frequency range, indicating they all have small diffusion resistances. Theequivalentseriesresistance are 0.86, 0.82 and 0.98Ωfor FDC-700, FDC-800 and FDC-900 respectively, and the contactresistance (semicircle diameter) of FDC-800 is only 0.18 Ω. The small resistance confirms that FDC-800 has good electrical conductivity, which promotes the charge transfer in electrolyte, leading to the high specific capacity of the FDC-800 one.

To further explain the good electrochemical performance of FDC-800, a series of characterizations are conducted to reveal its morphology and structural composition. SEM in Fig. 4e shows that FDC-800 is of fluffy lamellar structure with a lamellar thickness of 5 μm, and this structure increases the conductivity of sample and promotes chargetransferin electrolyte. The aerogel is consisted of large number of connected pore structures, exhibiting excellent hydrophilic property (inset of Fig. 4f), which can reduce the diffusion resistance when used in aqueous electrolyte [32]. From the HRTEM results (Fig. 4g), some short range ordered lattice structure can be observed with a latticing spacing of 0.34 nm. It shows FDC-800 has a certain degree of graphitization that can increase its conductivity. From the mapping images (Fig. 4h), C, N and O distributes evenly on FDC-800, confirming N is successfully doped into carbon skeleton after the carbonization.
CV curves at 10 mV/s(a), GCD at 0.2 A/g (b), Gravimetric capacitance at different current densities (c), EIS (d) of FDC-700, FDC-800 and FDC-900; SEM (e, f), HRTEM (g) and SEM mapping (h) of FDC-800.

From Nitrogen adsorption-desorption curves in Fig. 5 a, all the three samples show mixture of type I and type IV curves. A sharp increase can be observed when p/p0 < 0.01with hysteresis loop appeared at 0.4–1.0, denoting the existence of both micropores and mesopores [33]. The specific surface area of FDC-700 is only 180 m2/g with a S micro of 147 m2/g, indicating the 700 °C treatment mainly benefits for the formation of micropores. When increasing carbonization temperature to 800 °C, both micro and mesopore structure increases, leading to a SBET of 760 m2/g, but the micropores are still the dominant pore structure. High-temperature can promote the pore formation of carbon material, and carbon dioxide also contribute to the pore-forming and pore-expanding [34]. Further increasing carbonization temperature to 900 °C, the SBET further goes up to 772 m2/g, and the percentage of mesopores increases to 83%, indicating SBET of FDC-900 is mainly resulted from the mesopores. Carbonization at 900 °C is intense, and a large number of microporous structures collapse to form mesopores, leading to the increase of mesopore percentage. From Fig. 5(c), it is noticeable that the micro-meso pore size distribution of FDC-800 is at 0.5, 0.7 and 1.5 nm, while a broad distribution peak can be observed in 20–200 nm, indicating the presence of macropores. The hierarchical porous structure increases the wettability of FDC-800 as well as the contact area with the electrolyte, which further promotes the transport of the electrolyte and benefits for its electrochemical property [32].

The high-resolution XPS N1s of FDC-800 (Fig. 5d) is fitted into four peaks for pryridinic N (N-6, 398.1 eV), pyrrolic N (N-5, 400.3 eV), graphitic N (N-Q, 401.8 eV) and oxidized N (N-X, 403.2 eV) respectively. Pryridinic N and pyrrolic N are the main nitrogen-containing groups, such groups can provide pseudocapacitance directly, while the graphitic N can increase conductivity of FDC-800, which also enhances their electrochemical performance. The O1s in Fig. 5(e) is fitted into CO groups (O-Ⅰ, 531.1 eV), OCO groups (O-Ⅱ, 532.6 eV) and COH/COC groups (O-III, 533.7 eV), confirming abundant oxygen-containing groups existed on the surface of FDC-800. From Raman analysis in Fig. 5(f), the obvious peak centered at 1338 cm−1 is D band that originates from defective and disordered carbon, and the peak at 1590 cm−1 is related to the interlayer vibration of graphitic carbon [35]. ID /IG value is carried out to investigate the degree of order of FDC-800. The ID /IG value of 1.75 illustrates FDC-800 has a certain degree of graphitization, which is beneficial for the conductivity, agreeing well with TEM analysis above. On the other hand, the relatively high value denotes that they are still mainly disordered carbon, and these certain defective and disordered structure poses positive effect on charge transport [36].

To examine the practical application performance, two-electrode capacitance system is assembled using FDC-800 as electrodes. CV curves are all reversible rectangle-like shape at different scan rates, implying its excellent rate capability. The gravimetric capacitances are determined to be 208 F/g at 0.5 A/g and 182 F/g at 2 A/g. This ideal performance can be ascribed to the good transportation of electrolyte ions via micro, meso and macroporous structures through the aerogel structure electrodes. The ratio of the capacitance is determined by comparing the largest rectangle area with the remaining part from the CV curve as listed in Fig. 6 (c). Pseudocapacitance is calculated in this way as they vary with the applied voltage correspondingly [37]. For FDC-800, EDLC makes up for 86.1% while pseudocapacitance accounts for 13.9%, explaining the excellent performance of FDC-800. For EIS curve (Fig. 6d), the straight line at low frequency indicates FDC-800 has low diffusion resistance, due to they existence of abundant oxygen-containing groups on the surface, which increases hydrophilic property and decreases the diffusion resistance. The equivalentseriesresistance is 1.85 Ω, while the contactresistance (semicircle diameter) is only 0.49 Ω, indicating FDC-800 has rapid faradic reaction. From Fig. 6e, it can be seen the energy density reaches 7.2 W h/kg at a power density of 50 W/kg, and the energy density is 5.32 Wh/kg when the power density increased to 1000 W/kg. After long cycling at 5 A/g, FDC-800 retains 82.1% of the initial capacitance after 10,000 cycles (Fig. 6f).

Furthermore, FDC-800 is used as electrode to assemble symmetrical solid supercapacitor with lignin hydrogel solid electrolyte [38]. The obtained solid-state supercapacitor exhibits flexibility, as shown in Fig. 6(g), nearly no change is observed for the CV tests when the bending angles ranges from 0 to 150°. The device obtained by three electrodes connected in series shows similar charging and discharging time compared with the individual electrode, while the potential window is extended to 3.0 V, demonstrating the high capacitive stability of the solid-state supercapacitor (Fig. 6h). The LED lamp lighted up by the series device for few minutes (inset), furthering confirming that FDC-800 has promising potential using as flexible electrode in energy devices.

After impregnated with PdCl2 solution followed carbonization, the morphology of the obtained NACpd-C catalyst is characterized by SEM and TEM as shown in Fig. 7 . Different from FDC-T, NACpd-C shows irregular bulk structure with abundant pore channel connections, ascribed from the reactions occurred between metal salts and aerogel during the carbonization. Pd distributes evenly on NACs-C with an average size of 4.88 nm. From the HRTEM images, ordered lattice structures with lattice spaces of 0.195 nm and 0.225 nm are observed, corresponded to Pd (200) and Pd (111) respectively, indicating Pd has been successfully doped in carbon support [39].

The detailed structural composition of NACpd-C is further explored by XPS, Nitrogen adsorption-desorption, XRD, Pyridine IR and NH3-TPD, as depicted in Fig. 8 . From XPS results, the Pd peak can be resolved into four peaks, the peaks centered at 335.12 and 340.7 eV represent for Pd0, while the peaks at 336.1 and 341.9 eV are assigned to PdO. Pd0 is the main phase of the catalyst with a percentage accounting for 70%, indicating the preparation process is favorable to form metallic palladium. From Table S2, there is 4.73 at% of N on surface of NACpd-C, which is proved to prevent Pd0 from further oxidation in air [39]. The N1s peak can be fitted into three peaks at 397.8, 400.2 and 401.2 eV, representing for N-6, N-5 and N-Q respectively Fig. 8b. Such nitrogen-containing groups provide basic sites, promote charge transfer and enrich the electron density of the catalyst [40]. When used as catalyst for phenol hydrogenation, phenol is effectively adsorbed on catalyst due to the interactions of OH…N are formed between the phenol and amine groups of catalyst.

The porous characteristic of NACpd-C is exhibited in Fig. 8c. NACpd-C presents a mixture of micro and mesoporous structure with a specific surface area of 330 m2/g. Micropores dominates the pore structure with the S micro of 301 m2/g, and the pore size distribution are mainly centered at 0.8 and 1.2 nm. The specific surface area is much lower than that without pd loading (FDC-800), as Pd mainly loads in carbon skeleton and the surface of pore channels, leading to the decrease of both micro and mesopores. For XRD analysis in Fig. 8(d), a broad diffraction peaks, centered at 24.2°and assigned to 002 lattice spacing, is observed, indicating the characteristic of the amorphous carbon. Another three well defined diffraction peaks centered at 40.2, 46.7 and 68.3°are indexed (111), (200) and (220) reflections respectively (JCPDS 01-071-3757), representing for the Pd metal particles, which confirms the successfully doping of Pd in the metallic state [41]. A noticeable different result from XPS analysis is obtained with few PdO signal being observed, which probably due to their particle sizes are too small to be detected.

The acid type and acid amount of NACpd-C are characterized by Pyridine IR as shown in Fig. 8(e). The peaks centered at 1445 cm−1 and 1540 cm−1 represent for Lewis acids and Brønsted acids. Obviously, NACpd-C presents a clear Lewis acid peak, with the Lewis acid content of 56.02 umol/g (Table S2). There are a lot of oxygen-containing groups on the surface of NACpd-C, hydrogen bonds are consequently formed between the hydroxyl groups and the unsaturated double bonds, which endows the catalyst with electrophilicity and therefore lead to Lewis acidity [42]. On the other hand, interaction between Pd and the acid sites occurs to form an “electron-deficient” structure, which can also provide the Lewis acidity [43]. NH3-TPD is used to evaluate the acid strength and acidity of the catalyst as listed in Fig. 8f. Two obvious peaks are observed. The peak centered below 200 belongs to the physically adsorbed ammonia, the other peak between 200 and 500 is ascribed to medium-strength acid centers, implying the acid on NACpd-C is mainly medium-strength acid.

NACPd-C is used as catalyst for phenol hydrogenation, and the results are presented in Table 1 . Almost no conversion of phenol occurs without adding catalyst or using NAC-C, due to no catalytic active sites existed for the reaction. When NACPd-C is used as catalyst, phenol is highly efficient converted, with cyclohexanone and cyclohexanol being the only products. Based on our previous study, 100°C is an appropriate temperature for this reaction, thus it is also employed for this investigation. Firstly, the effect of reaction time on catalytic activity is studied. 53.8% conversion of phenol, with cyclohexanone selectivity of 96.2% is achieved at 100 °C, 1 h and 1 MPa condition, verifying the catalyst has catalytic activity for the reaction. When increasing the reaction time from 1 h to 3 h, the conversion of phenol increases to 77.7%. Keep increasing the reaction time to 5 h, 97.3% of the conversion with cyclohexanone selectivity of 97.8% is obtained. Further rising the reaction time up to 6 h, although the conversion can reach 100%, the selectivity decreases to 95.8%, which probably due to the reaction time is too long, and cyclohexanone further hydrogenated to form cyclohexanol. When holding reaction time at 5 h, increasing pressure to 2 MPa leads to the highest selectivity of 98.3%, while reacting at 3 MPa results in decrease of the selectivity, indicating high pressure can push the reaction forward, but on the other hand, it also facilitates the cyclohexanone hydrogenation at the same time. As a result, 100 °C, 5 h and 2 MPa is selected as the optimized reaction parameters.

Based on the above analysis, the reaction mechanism of phenol hydrogenation adopting NACPd-C as catalyst is described as follow: the nitrogen-containing groups on carbon support provide basic sites and adsorb phenol on the catalyst for further reactions. Study has confirmed that phenol adsorbed on basic sites benefit for the generation of cyclohexanone instead of cyclohexanol [40]. The abundant micropores of catalyst supplies sufficient contact surface area for phenol, and the mesopores provide catalytic reaction space. Pd could active H2 during the reaction [44]. and the activated H then undergoes the cycloaddition reaction of phenol with the action of Lewis acid for cyclohexanone. The generated:OC on cyclohexanone bonds with Lewis acid via acid-base interaction, which can stabilize the cyclohexanone and inhibit their further hydrogenation to cyclohexanol [45]. Leading to high selectivity for cyclohexanone.

The recycling experiments of NACPd-C after five-time recycling are illustrated in Figure S3. The conversions of phenol to cyclohexanone are quantitative calculated. The catalyst still exhibits high catalytic activity with selectivity higher than 97.1%, demonstrating the good reusability. In this study, the active sites Pd n
+ is doped in carbon skeleton, leading to the high stability of catalyst. After used for five times, the specific surface area of NACPd-C decreases to 168 m2/g, mainly ascribed to the decrease of micropores as shown in TableS3, which indicates that the slight decrease of selectivity may be derived from the decrease of porosity.

Carbon aerogels with various applications are prepared from chitosan and glycine hydrochloride in this study. NACs-Q is water-soluble quantum dot with average particle sizes of 3.8 nm, presenting excitation-/emission-independent (at wavelengths of 340/432 nm) and pH-sensitive properties. By chelating the surface OH and NH2 groups with Fe3+, NACs-Q presents high sensitivity (detection limit of 0.89 μM) and selectivity to Fe3+ in aqueous solution, which enable them acting as an “on-off-on” sensor for detections of Fe3+ and vitamin C in fruits. Moreover, after compositing NACs-Q with PVA, the obtained film shows good PL responses to acetone vapor with a detection limit of 1.44 μM.

Abundant oxygen and nitrogen-containing groups existed on the surfaces of FDC-800 generally benefit pseudocapacitance, and the three-electrode system employing FDC-800 as electrode delivers specific capacitances of 216 F/g at 10 A/g. The outstanding capacitance is mainly derived from the combination of EDLC and pseudocapacitance. Besides, when it was adopted into a flexible solid-state supercapacitor using lignin hydrogel electrolytes, capacitance of 208 F/g at 0.5 A/g, and an energy density of 7.2 Wh/kg at a power density of 50 W/kg can be achieved, evidencing the promising potential for FDC-800 applied in flexible energy devices.

NACPd-C exhibits excellent catalytic effect for phenol hydrogenation, providing a phenol conversion of 100% and cyclohexanone selectivity of 98.3%. The good catalytic performances result from the synergetic effects of the Pd particles, the N-functional groups, and the Lewis acid sites on the support. The N-containing groups such as N-5 and N-6 groups on NACPd-C provide basic sites to adsorb phenol effectively. Pd in the as-prepared catalyst mainly presents in the form of Pd0, which could activate H2 effectively during the reaction. Carbon support has intrinsic Lewis acid sites due to its electrophilicity, and the Lewis acid sites promote the conversion of phenol and interact with the cyclohexanone product via its oxygen lone pair to inhibit further conversion, providing high cyclohexanone selectivity.
Overall, this study provides novel directions for preparation and application of functional carbon aerogel from biomass.
